skill.name: dependency-audit-assistant
skill.description: Audits project dependencies for known vulnerabilities, outdated packages, dependency tree risks, and license compliance issues.
skill.summary: Find dependency security, version, and license risks before release.
skill.author: CrazyDubya

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high and critical hits, but most are false positives from reference documentation that lists unsafe dependency patterns as examples. The confirmed risk is a bundled Bash helper that runs package manager tools, may install audit utilities from registries, and writes a predictable temporary file. No prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent was found, so publication is acceptable with a warning.

medium_findings.title: Helper Script Installs and Runs External Package Tools
medium_findings.description: The bundled Bash helper checks for license audit tools and installs missing tools with npm, pip, or go before running them. This is expected for a dependency audit skill, but it can modify the user environment and retrieve code from package registries.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The install and execution commands are explicit in the script. The behavior is legitimate for license auditing, but it creates supply-chain and environment modification risk.

medium_findings.title: Broad External Command Guidance for Dependency Audits
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to run many package manager and audit commands across ecosystems. These commands are appropriate for the stated purpose, but should be run only in trusted workspaces or after user approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions clearly direct use of external tooling. I found no command injection intent, but package manager commands can contact registries and inspect local project files.

low_findings.title: Predictable Temporary File Path
low_findings.description: The npm license path writes results to /tmp/licenses.json and later removes that same path. This is a minor local safety issue because a fixed temporary path can collide with another process or symlink on shared systems.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The fixed temporary path is directly visible. Impact is limited because the file contains license output and the script runs with the current user privileges.

low_findings.title: Static Documentation Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The critical and high static hits for exec, pickle, weak cryptography, malware keywords, and hardcoded URLs appear in reference documentation describing common vulnerabilities and resources. They are examples for audit guidance, not executable malicious code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced files are Markdown guidance and examples. I found no evidence that these snippets are executed by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automatic Installation from Package Registries
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper script can install license audit tools if they are missing. Users should review this behavior before running the script in sensitive or locked-down environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are direct install commands. The risk depends on registry trust and local execution policy, not on confirmed malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell-Based License Collection
dangerous_patterns.description: The license helper uses shell commands and pipelines to collect package metadata. This is normal for audit automation, but shell execution should be limited to trusted repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell usage is explicit and mostly bounded to known tools. I did not find direct unsanitized user command construction.

content.value_statement: Dependency risk is hard to track across package managers, advisories, and license rules. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through focused audits with practical remediation steps.
actual_capabilities: Detects common package managers from project files and lock files.
Guides security audits with ecosystem tools such as npm audit, pip-audit, cargo audit, and govulncheck.
Categorizes outdated dependencies by patch, minor, and major update risk.
Reviews dependency licenses against permissive, weak copyleft, strong copyleft, and unknown categories.
Summarizes direct and transitive dependency risks with prioritized remediation actions.
Provides reference material for common dependency vulnerabilities and license compatibility.
limitations: It depends on local package manager tools being installed and working.
It does not replace legal review for complex license questions.
It cannot prove exploitability without application context and testing.
The helper script may install audit utilities unless the user reviews or edits it first.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Release Security Check
use_cases.description: Run a final dependency review before deployment and prioritize critical fixes.

use_cases.title: Review Open Source License Exposure
use_cases.description: Identify GPL, AGPL, unknown, or custom licenses that need policy review.

use_cases.title: Triage Dependency Update Work
use_cases.description: Separate safe patch updates from major upgrades that require migration testing.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this project for dependency vulnerabilities, outdated packages, and license issues. Summarize the most important actions first.

prompt_templates.title: Release Gate Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a dependency audit for this release candidate. Identify blockers, recommended fixes, and items that can wait until after release.

prompt_templates.title: License Compliance Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review dependency licenses for a commercial product. Flag GPL, AGPL, unknown, and custom licenses with recommended next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Prioritized Remediation Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a dependency remediation plan across all package managers in this repository. Group findings by severity, exposure, update risk, and owner action.

output_examples.input: Audit a Node.js service before deployment.
output: Summary: one high vulnerability, three moderate vulnerabilities, and twelve outdated packages were found.
Immediate action: update the affected production dependency and rerun the test suite.
Follow-up: review two major version upgrades separately because they may include breaking changes.

output_examples.input: Check whether dependencies are safe for a proprietary SaaS product.
output: License review: most packages use permissive licenses.
Concern: one AGPL package and two unknown licenses require legal or replacement review.
Recommendation: replace the AGPL package before release or obtain formal approval.

output_examples.input: Prioritize dependency updates for a mixed Python and Go repository.
output: Priority 1: fix vulnerable production dependencies with available patches.
Priority 2: update stale direct dependencies with low migration risk.
Priority 3: schedule major upgrades that require compatibility testing.

best_practices: Run audits in a clean branch and review commands before allowing package installs.
Commit lock files and rerun tests after each security or dependency update.
Document accepted risks with owner, reason, expiry date, and review plan.
anti_patterns: Do not run audit commands blindly in untrusted repositories.
Do not treat a clean scanner result as proof that dependencies are safe.
Do not ignore license findings because the package is only transitive.

faq.question: Does this skill fix vulnerabilities automatically?
faq.answer: It recommends fixes and commands. Users should review changes and run tests before applying updates.

faq.question: Can it audit more than one package manager?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for JavaScript, Python, Ruby, Java, Go, Rust, and PHP ecosystems.

faq.question: Does it require internet access?
faq.answer: Many audit and update checks contact package registries or advisory databases. Offline results may be incomplete.

faq.question: Is the license guidance legal advice?
faq.answer: No. It provides engineering guidance and should be reviewed by legal counsel for business decisions.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill includes a Bash helper that can install audit tools and run package commands. This is useful but needs user awareness.

faq.question: Can it assess exploitability?
faq.answer: It can help triage exposure, but exploitability depends on how the application uses each dependency.
